subject: ap-calculus-bc
title: Polar Curves — Area, Tangent Line, Related Rates
stimulus: The graphs of the polar curves $r = 4$ and $r = 3 + 2\cos\theta$ are shown in the figure above. The curves intersect at $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{3}$ and $\theta = \dfrac{5\pi}{3}$.

parts.prompt: Let $R$ be the shaded region that is inside the graph of $r = 4$ and also outside the graph of $r = 3 + 2\cos\theta$, as shown in the figure above. Write an expression involving one or more integrals for the area of $R$.

scoring_points.description: Correct integrand using the polar area formula: $\dfrac{1}{2}\left[(4)^2 - (3 + 2\cos\theta)^2\right] = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[16 - (3 + 2\cos\theta)^2\right]$
Correct limits of integration: from $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{3}$ to $\theta = \dfrac{5\pi}{3}$, which is the interval where $r = 4 > r = 3 + 2\cos\theta$ (equivalently $\cos\theta < \dfrac{1}{2}$).

parts.prompt: Find the slope of the line tangent to the graph of $r = 3 + 2\cos\theta$ at $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$.

scoring_points.description:
Correctly expresses slope using the parametric form $x = r\cos\theta$ and $y = r\sin\theta$ with $r = 3 + 2\cos\theta$, so $\dfrac{dr}{d\theta} = -2\sin\theta$. Computes:
$$\frac{dy}{d\theta} = \frac{dr}{d\theta}\sin\theta + r\cos\theta = -2\sin^2\theta + (3+2\cos\theta)\cos\theta$$
$$\frac{dx}{d\theta} = \frac{dr}{d\theta}\cos\theta - r\sin\theta = -2\sin\theta\cos\theta - (3+2\cos\theta)\sin\theta$$
Correct slope of the tangent line: $\dfrac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{\theta=\pi/2} = \dfrac{-2}{-3} = \dfrac{2}{3}$

parts.prompt: A particle moves along the portion of the curve $r = 3 + 2\cos\theta$ for $0 < \theta < \pi$. The particle moves in such a way that the distance from the particle to the origin increases at a constant rate of $3$ units per second. Find the rate at which the angle $\theta$ changes with respect to time at the instant when the position of the particle corresponds to $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{3}$. Indicate units of measure.

scoring_points.description: Recognizes that the distance from the particle to the origin is $r = 3 + 2\cos\theta$, so $\dfrac{dr}{dt} = 3$ (given). Differentiates $r$ with respect to $t$: $\dfrac{dr}{dt} = \dfrac{dr}{d\theta}\cdot\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = -2\sin\theta\cdot\dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$.

Correct answer with units: $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = -\dfrac{3}{\sqrt{3}} = -\sqrt{3}$ radians per second. The negative value indicates that $\theta$ is decreasing as the particle moves along $0 < \theta < \pi$ with the distance from the origin increasing.
